title: As to the passage “That in the ages to come &c.“

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: A third passage with which he finds fault is that in which I gave a threefold interpretation of the Apostle’s words: “That in the ages to come he might shew the exceeding riches of his grace in kindness towards us in Christ Jesus.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The first was my own opinion, the second the opposite opinion held by Origen, the third the simple explanation given by Apollinarius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to the fact that I did not give their names, I must ask for pardon on the ground that it was done through modesty.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I did not wish to disparage men whom I was partly following. and whose opinions I was translating into the Latin tongue.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, I said, the diligent reader will at once search into these things and form his own opinion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And I repeated at the end: Another turns to a different sense the words ‘That in the ages to come he might shew the exceeding riches of his grace.’

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “Ah,” you will say, “I see that in the character of the diligent reader you have unfolded the opinions of Origen.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I confess that I was wrong.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I ought to have said not The diligent but The blasphemous reader.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If I had anticipated that you would adopt measures of this kind I might have done this, and so have avoided your calumnious speeches.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is, I suppose, a great crime to have called Origen a diligent reader, especially when I had translated seventy books of his and had praised him up to the sky,—for doing which I had to defend myself in a short treatise two years ago in answer to your trumpeting of my praises.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In those ‘praises’ which you gave me you laid it to my charge that I had spoken of Origen as a teacher of the churches, and now that you speak in the character of an enemy you think that I shall be afraid because you accuse me of calling him a diligent reader.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Why, even shopkeepers who are particularly frugal, and slaves who are not wasteful, and the care-takers who made our childhood a burden to us and even thieves when they are particularly clever, we speak of as diligent; and so the conduct of the unjust steward in the Gospel is spoken of as wise.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Moreover “The children of this world are wiser than the children of light,” and “The serpent was wiser than all the beasts which the Lord had made on the earth.”
